title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: ensembleHTE package provides comprehensive framework learning features heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) randomized controlled trials (RCTs) using ensemble machine learning methods. package implements estimation strategy developed Fava (2025), combines predictions multiple machine learning algorithms using Best Linear Predictor (BLP) weights repeated K-fold cross-fitting improve statistical power. key innovations existing methods using entire sample final inference rather holding data validation, combining multiple machine learning algorithms improve predictive performance.

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: package designed researchers practitioners want : Detect treatment effect heterogeneity: Test whether treatment affects different individuals differently based observable characteristics Characterize beneficiaries: Identify characteristics groups individuals benefit (less) treatment Validate predictions: Test whether machine learning predictions genuine --sample predictive power Predict outcomes: Use ensemble ML standard prediction tasks without treatment Compare targeting strategies: Evaluate whether unrestricted targeting (rank everyone) outperforms constrained targeting (rank within subgroups)

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: randomized controlled trials (RCTs), Average Treatment Effect (ATE) measures mean effect treatment across population: ATE=E[Yi(1)−Yi(0)],\text{ATE} = E[Y_i(1) - Y_i(0)], Yi(1)Y_i(1) Yi(0)Y_i(0) potential outcomes individual ii treatment control, respectively. However, treatment effects often vary across individuals. Conditional Average Treatment Effect (CATE) captures heterogeneity: CATE(x)=τ(x)=E[Yi(1)−Yi(0)|Xi=x]\text{CATE}(x) = \tau(x) = E[Y_i(1) - Y_i(0) | X_i = x] XiX_i represents individual characteristics (covariates).

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: GATES (Chernozhukov et al., 2025) provides structured way test visualize treatment effect heterogeneity : Using machine learning predict individual treatment effects τ̂\hat{\tau}_i based covariates Sorting individuals quantile groups (e.g., terciles) based predictions Estimating average treatment effect within group Testing whether high-prediction low-prediction groups statistically different effects

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: package improves upon existing single-algorithm approaches : Combining Multiple ML Algorithms: Uses several algorithms (e.g., random forests, gradient boosting, elastic net) optimally combines predictions using BLP weights K-fold Cross-Fitting: Trains models K-1 folds generates predictions held-fold Full-Sample Inference: Uses entire dataset final estimation, maximizing statistical power Repeated Sample-Splitting: Averages across M repetitions stability valid inference. Important: debugging exploration, use M = 2 3 quickly verify code works. final results publication, use M ≥ 100 least (use can!). tests conducted methodology recommended usage require M ≥ 100.

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: Install development version GitHub: package requires R ≥ 4.0.0 depends several packages including grf, mlr3, data.table, ggplot2. installed automatically. additional ML algorithms, may want install optional packages: recommended frequently check package updates:
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("bfava/ensembleHTE") install.packages(c("glmnet", "gbm", "ranger")) ensemble_news()

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: Throughout vignette, use microcredit dataset ships package. contains data randomized microfinance experiment Philippines microloans randomly offered applicants. gentler, application-oriented introduction package using dataset, see companion article Introduction ensembleHTE. article walks practical analysis step step, present guide provides comprehensive coverage every feature option available package. Key variables: treat: treatment indicator (1 = loan offered) prop_score: known propensity score (varies credit-score window) exp_yrly_end: yearly business expenses endline (pesos) hhinc_yrly_end: yearly household income endline (pesos) bank_profits_pp: bank profit per peso lent (observed borrowers)
code: library(ensembleHTE) data(microcredit)  dim(microcredit) #> [1] 1113   51 head(microcredit[, c("treat", "prop_score", "exp_yrly_end", "hhinc_yrly_end")]) #>   treat prop_score exp_yrly_end hhinc_yrly_end #> 1     1  0.8434874        29475          15750 #> 2     1  0.5465839        11760          66990 #> 3     1  0.8434874         1800           9450 #> 4     1  0.8434874          975          29025 #> 5     1  0.8434874         9000           3750 #> 6     1  0.8434874            0              0  # Variables used throughout this guide hte_covars <- c("css_creditscorefinal", "lower_window", "own_anybus",                 "max_yearsinbusiness", "css_assetvalue") profit_covars <- c("css_creditscorefinal", "own_anybus", "css_assetvalue",                    "age", "gender", "hhinc_yrly_base") has_loan <- microcredit$treat == 1 & microcredit$loan_size > 0  # Income quintiles (used for restricted comparisons later) microcredit$hhinc_quintile <- cut(   microcredit$hhinc_yrly_base,   breaks = quantile(microcredit$hhinc_yrly_base, probs = seq(0, 1, by = 0.2)),   labels = paste0("Q", 1:5),   include.lowest = TRUE )

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: main function HTE estimation ensemble_hte(): print() method shows: Data dimensions specification Algorithms used ensemble Metalearner strategy (R-learner default) Split-sample parameters (M repetitions, K folds) detailed output: summary() method provides comprehensive overview including: BLP Analysis: Estimates beta1 (ATE) beta2 (HET) standard errors p-values, plus interpretation whether significant heterogeneity detected GATES Analysis: Group average treatment effects showing estimates quantile group, along key hypothesis tests (Top-Bottom, Top-)
code: # Fit ensemble HTE model # Start with M = 2 for quick debugging, then increase to M >= 100 for final results fit <- ensemble_hte(   Y = "exp_yrly_end",   D = "treat",   X = hte_covars,   data       = microcredit,   prop_score = "prop_score",   algorithms = c("lm", "grf"),  # Linear model + causal forest   M = 2,                         # Use M = 2-3 for debugging, M >= 100 for final results   K = 3                          # 3-fold cross-fitting )  # View basic information print(fit) #> Ensemble HTE Fit #> ================ #>  #> Call: #> ensemble_hte(data = microcredit, Y = "exp_yrly_end", X = hte_covars,  #>     D = "treat", prop_score = "prop_score", M = 2, K = 3, algorithms = c("lm",  #>         "grf")) #>  #> Data: #>   Observations:      1113 #>   Targeted outcome:  exp_yrly_end #>   Treatment:         treat #>   Covariates:        5 #>  #> Model specification: #>   Algorithms:        lm, grf #>   Metalearner:       R-learner #>   Task type:         regression (continuous outcome) #>   R-learner method:  grf #>  #> Split-sample parameters: #>   Repetitions (M):   2 #>   Folds (K):         3 #>   Ensemble strategy: cross-validated BLP #>   Ensemble folds:    5 #>   Covariate scaling: enabled #>   Hyperparameter tuning: disabled summary(fit) #> Ensemble HTE Summary #> ==================== #>  #> Call: #> ensemble_hte(data = microcredit, Y = "exp_yrly_end", X = hte_covars,  #>     D = "treat", prop_score = "prop_score", M = 2, K = 3, algorithms = c("lm",  #>         "grf")) #>  #> Outcome:     exp_yrly_end #> Treatment:   treat #> Observations: 1113 #> Repetitions:  2 #>  #> Best Linear Predictor (BLP): #>   beta1 (ATE):       -2244.44 (SE: 4317.52, p: 0.603)  #>   beta2 (HET):       0.65 (SE: 0.35, p: 0.064) . #>   -> No significant heterogeneity detected (p >= 0.05) #>  #> Group Average Treatment Effects (GATES) with 3 groups: #>   Group    Estimate   Std.Error    Pr(>|t|) #>   -------------------------------------------- #>       1   -15486.55     9344.43       0.097 . #>       2     4685.47     5731.44       0.414  #>       3     4533.65     6527.62       0.487  #>  #>   Top - Bottom:  20020.20 (SE: 11388.96, p: 0.079) . #>  #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: ite() function extracts matrix individualized treatment effect predictions fitted HTE model: column corresponds one repetition cross-fitting procedure. analysis beyond package, recommended average ITE estimates across repetitions, invalidate inference. Instead, run analysis separately column average results across repetitions.
code: # Extract ITE matrix: n observations x M repetitions ite_matrix <- ite(fit) dim(ite_matrix) #> [1] 1113    2

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: gates() function computes average treatment effects groups defined predicted ITE quantiles:
code: # Compute GATES with 3 groups (terciles) gates_results <- gates(fit, n_groups = 3) print(gates_results) #> GATES Results #> ============= #>  #> Fit type: HTE (ensemble_hte) #> Outcome analyzed: exp_yrly_end #> Number of groups: 3 #> Repetitions: 2 #>  #> Group Average Treatment Effects: #>  #>   Group    Estimate   Std.Error   t value    Pr(>|t|) #>   ---------------------------------------------------- #>       1   -15486.55     9344.43     -1.66       0.097 . #>       2     4685.47     5731.44      0.82       0.414  #>       3     4533.65     6527.62      0.69       0.487  #>  #> Heterogeneity Tests: #>   ---------------------------------------------------- #>           Test    Estimate   Std.Error   t value    Pr(>|t|) #>   ---------------------------------------------------- #>     Top-Bottom    20020.20    11388.96      1.76       0.079 . #>        Top-All     6641.05     5701.03      1.16       0.244  #>  #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

previous_headings: Analyzing Treatment Effect Heterogeneity > Group Average Treatment Effects (GATES)
title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: output includes: Group Estimates: Group 1: Bottom tercile (lowest predicted ITEs) Group 2: Middle tercile Group 3: Top tercile (highest predicted ITEs) group shows estimated average treatment effect individuals group, along standard errors, t-statistics, p-values. Heterogeneity Tests: Top-Bottom: Tests whether top group different average treatment effect bottom group. significant result (p < 0.05) indicates evidence heterogeneity. Top-: Tests whether top group differs overall average. Useful comparing targeting strategies focusing high-benefit individuals versus treating everyone.

text: GATES estimates showing treatment effect heterogeneity plot shows: Point estimates group 95% confidence intervals Blue line indicating overall ATE Groups ordered lowest highest predicted benefit confidence intervals don’t overlap much (especially groups 1 3), provides visual evidence heterogeneity.
code: plot(gates_results)

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: blp() function tests whether ML predictions capture meaningful heterogeneity:
code: # Compute BLP blp_results <- blp(fit) print(blp_results) #> BLP Results (Best Linear Predictor of CATE) #> ============================================ #>  #> Fit type: HTE (ensemble_hte) #> Outcome analyzed: exp_yrly_end #> Repetitions: 2 #>  #> Coefficients: #>   beta1 (ATE): Average Treatment Effect #>   beta2 (HET): Heterogeneity loading (significant = ML captures heterogeneity) #>  #>     Term    Estimate   Std.Error   t value    Pr(>|t|) #>   ---------------------------------------------------- #>    beta1    -2244.44     4317.52     -0.52       0.603  #>    beta2        0.65        0.35      1.85       0.064 . #>  #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

previous_headings: Analyzing Treatment Effect Heterogeneity > Best Linear Predictor (BLP)
title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: BLP regression : Yi=β1(Di−pi)+β2(Di−pi)(τ̂−τ̂‾)+ϵiY_i = \beta_1 (D_i - p_i) + \beta_2 (D_i - p_i)(\hat{\tau}_i - \bar{\hat{\tau}}) + \epsilon_i pip_i propensity score (probability treatment), τ̂‾\bar{\hat{\tau}} average predicted treatment effect. Coefficients: beta1 (ATE): Estimates overall average treatment effect. beta2 (HET): Tests heterogeneity. significant beta2 (p < 0.05) indicates ML predictions τ̂\hat{\tau}_i capture genuine treatment effect heterogeneity. beta2 ≈ 1, predictions well-calibrated. can also add control variables BLP regression using controls parameter:
code: # BLP with additional control variables blp_with_controls <- blp(fit, controls = c("age", "gender"))

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: analysis functions (gates(), gavs(), clan(), gates_restricted(), gavs_restricted()) accept subset argument restricts observations used analysis. subset can logical vector, integer index vector, string "train" (use training subset train_idx) "". subset specified, observations enter analysis (e.g., CLAN computes group means subset; GATES runs regressions subset). Separately, group_on argument controls observations used define group cutoffs (.e., quantile thresholds assign individuals groups). distinct subset: subset determines analyzed, group_on determines whose predictions define group boundaries fall. group_on = "auto" (default): Groups formed using population used train ML model. ensemble_hte ensemble_pred fit without train_idx, means observations; trained subset (via train_idx), means training subset. default ensures group definitions reflect population ML model designed . group_on = "": Groups always formed using observations, regardless ML trained subset analyzed. group_on = "analysis": Groups formed using observations analysis subset. means group boundaries re-computed within subset, individuals may assigned different groups full sample.
code: # Suppose you want to analyze CLAN only for business owners subset_biz <- microcredit$own_anybus == 1  # Default (auto): group cutoffs come from the ML training population clan_all <- clan(fit, n_groups = 3, subset = subset_biz)  # Alternative: form groups only within the business-owner subset clan_reranked <- clan(fit, n_groups = 3, subset = subset_biz,                       group_on = "analysis")

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: metalearner parameter ensemble_hte() controls strategy estimating individual treatment effects. package supports four strategies:

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: R-learner (Nie & Wager, 2021) uses residual--residual regression: Estimate conditional mean m̂(x)=E[Y|X=x]\hat{m}(x) = E[Y|X=x] using specified base algorithm Compute residuals: Ỹ=Yi−m̂(Xi)\tilde{Y}_i = Y_i - \hat{m}(X_i) D̃=Di−pi\tilde{D}_i = D_i - p_i Estimate CATE τ̂(x)\hat{\tau}(x) regressing Ỹ\tilde{Y} D̃\tilde{D} (via causal forest weighted regression) Note propensity scores: original R-learner framework (Robinson, 1988; Nie & Wager, 2021) estimates propensity score e(x)=E[D|X]e(x) = E[D|X] nuisance parameter. Since package designed RCTs, propensity score known design, implementation uses known propensity score pip_i directly rather estimating . avoids unnecessary estimation step uses exact treatment assignment probabilities experimental protocol. r_learner parameter controls algorithm used CATE estimation step. default, uses "grf" (causal forest), mlr3 learner can specified:
code: fit_r <- ensemble_hte(   Y = "exp_yrly_end",   D = "treat",   X = hte_covars,   data       = microcredit,   prop_score = "prop_score",   metalearner = "r",   r_learner = "grf",  # Use causal forest for CATE estimation (default)   M = 100, K = 3  # Use M >= 100 for final results )

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: Trains separate models treated control groups: τ̂(x)=μ̂1(x)−μ̂0(x)\hat{\tau}(x) = \hat{\mu}_1(x) - \hat{\mu}_0(x)
code: fit_t <- ensemble_hte(   Y = "exp_yrly_end",   D = "treat",   X = hte_covars,   data       = microcredit,   prop_score = "prop_score",   metalearner = "t",   M = 100, K = 3 )

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: Trains single model treatment feature: μ̂(x,d)=E[Y|X=x,D=d]\hat{\mu}(x, d) = E[Y | X=x, D=d]τ̂(x)=μ̂(x,1)−μ̂(x,0)\hat{\tau}(x) = \hat{\mu}(x, 1) - \hat{\mu}(x, 0)
code: fit_s <- ensemble_hte(   Y = "exp_yrly_end",   D = "treat",   X = hte_covars,   data       = microcredit,   prop_score = "prop_score",   metalearner = "s",   M = 100, K = 3 )

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: two-stage approach (Künzel et al., 2019) imputes counterfactual outcomes: Recommendation: R-learner r_learner = "grf" generally good default. T-learner works well sufficient sample sizes arm. S-learner can underestimate heterogeneity base learner struggles detect treatment-covariate interactions.
code: fit_x <- ensemble_hte(   Y = "exp_yrly_end",   D = "treat",   X = hte_covars,   data       = microcredit,   prop_score = "prop_score",   metalearner = "x",   M = 100, K = 3 )

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: applications, recommend using 3-4 algorithms (5). good general-purpose ensemble: combines: glmnet: Elastic Net grf: Generalized Random Forest nnet: Neural Network xgboost: Gradient Boosting
code: # Good general-purpose ensemble (4 algorithms) fit <- ensemble_hte(   Y = "exp_yrly_end",   D = "treat",   X = hte_covars,   data       = microcredit,   prop_score = "prop_score",   algorithms = c("glmnet", "grf", "nnet", "xgboost"),   M = 3, K = 3  # Use M >= 100 for final results )

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: Hyperparameter tuning applies mlr3 learners (e.g., "ranger", "glmnet", "xgboost"). "grf" algorithm uses built-tuning affected tune parameter. Enable automatic tuning setting tune = TRUE. function sensible defaults, additional parameters required:
code: # Simple usage with defaults fit_tuned <- ensemble_hte(   Y = "exp_yrly_end",   D = "treat",   X = hte_covars,   data       = microcredit,   prop_score = "prop_score",   algorithms = c("ranger", "glmnet"),   tune = TRUE,  # Just set this to TRUE - defaults handle the rest   M = 3, K = 3 )

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: can customize built-tuning process passing list scalar parameters via tune_params:
code: # Customized tuning parameters (optional) fit_tuned <- ensemble_hte(   Y = "exp_yrly_end",   D = "treat",   X = hte_covars,   data       = microcredit,   prop_score = "prop_score",   algorithms = c("ranger", "glmnet"),   tune = TRUE,   tune_params = list(     time = 60,              # Max tuning time in seconds per algorithm (default: 30)     cv_folds = 3,           # Number of CV folds for tuning (default: 3)     stagnation_iters = 250, # Stop if no improvement for this many iterations (default: 250)     stagnation_threshold = 0.01  # Minimum improvement threshold (default: 0.01)   ),   M = 3, K = 3 )

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: full control tuning process, can pass mlr3tuning objects directly. lets specify custom tuner, terminator, resampling strategy, search space, measure: See mlr3tuning documentation details available tuners, terminators, search spaces. Note: Tuning significantly increases computation time. exploratory analysis, use tune = FALSE enable tuning final results.
code: # Advanced: pass mlr3tuning objects for full control fit_tuned <- ensemble_hte(   Y = "exp_yrly_end",   D = "treat",   X = hte_covars,   data       = microcredit,   prop_score = "prop_score",   algorithms = c("ranger", "glmnet"),   tune = TRUE,   tune_params = list(     tuner       = mlr3tuning::tnr("grid_search", resolution = 5),     terminator  = mlr3tuning::trm("evals", n_evals = 50),     resampling  = mlr3::rsmp("holdout"),     measure     = "regr.rsq"   ),   M = 3, K = 3 )

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: can pass algorithm-specific hyperparameters using learner_params argument. applies mlr3 learners ignored "grf" (uses internal defaults). argument named list element named algorithm contains list parameter-value pairs. parameter names follow mlr3 parameter naming convention — see parameters available given learner, run mlr3::lrn("regr.<algorithm>")$param_set: values override algorithm-specific defaults without invoking tuning machinery. Useful already know good hyperparameter values data.
code: fit <- ensemble_hte(   Y = "exp_yrly_end",   D = "treat",   X = hte_covars,   data       = microcredit,   prop_score = "prop_score",   algorithms = c("ranger", "xgboost"),   learner_params = list(     ranger  = list(num.trees = 500, min.node.size = 5),     xgboost = list(nrounds = 200, max_depth = 4)   ),   M = 3, K = 3 )

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: default, package combines algorithm predictions using BLP weights estimated via cross-validation (ensemble_strategy = "cv"). can switch simple averaging: cross-validation BLP strategy ("cv", default) generally preferred, learns data-adaptive weights upweight better-performing algorithms.
code: # Simple average of all algorithm predictions (equal weights) fit_avg <- ensemble_hte(   Y = "exp_yrly_end",   D = "treat",   X = hte_covars,   data       = microcredit,   prop_score = "prop_score",   algorithms = c("lm", "grf", "ranger"),   ensemble_strategy = "average",  # Equal-weighted average instead of BLP   M = 3, K = 3 )

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: package designed randomized controlled trials (RCTs), propensity score (probability treatment) known design. Default behavior: don’t specify prop_score, function assumes constant propensity equal sample treatment proportion. appropriate simple randomization equal assignment probabilities. specify prop_score: need provide prop_score assignment probability varies across individuals. everyone probability (even 50%), default correctly use sample treatment fraction: Important: Since package RCTs, always know propensity scores experimental design. estimate propensity scores — use true assignment probabilities randomization protocol.
code: # For standard 50/50 randomization, no need to specify prop_score fit <- ensemble_hte(   Y = "exp_yrly_end",   D = "treat",   X = hte_covars,   data = microcredit,   M = 3, K = 3 )  # Default: prop_score = mean(D) for all observations # In the microcredit experiment, propensity scores vary by credit-score window. # Pass the column name to use them: fit <- ensemble_hte(   Y = "exp_yrly_end",   D = "treat",   X = hte_covars,   data       = microcredit,   prop_score = "prop_score",  # Required when probabilities vary   M = 3, K = 3 )

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: default, covariates standardized passed ML algorithms (scale_covariates = TRUE). Two details worth noting: Binary variables (columns 0 1 values) never scaled, since scaling remove natural interpretation Scaling fit training data : using train_idx, scaling parameters (mean standard deviation) computed training observations applied observations, preventing data leakage Scaling used ML training prediction: standardized covariates used internally train models generate predictions. downstream analysis functions (gates(), blp(), clan(), gavs(), etc.) operate original, unscaled data. example, CLAN reports group means original units variable. Factor character columns automatically handled: converted factors , algorithms require numeric input (grf), one-hot encoded using dummy variables. can disable scaling covariates already comparable scales:
code: fit <- ensemble_hte(   Y = "exp_yrly_end",   D = "treat",   X = hte_covars,   data       = microcredit,   prop_score = "prop_score",   scale_covariates = FALSE,  # Disable automatic scaling   M = 3, K = 3 )

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: Speed computation parallelizing across repetitions:
code: # Use 4 cores for faster computation fit <- ensemble_hte(   Y = "exp_yrly_end",   D = "treat",   X = hte_covars,   data       = microcredit,   prop_score = "prop_score",   algorithms = c("lm", "grf"),   M = 100,   K = 3,   n_cores = 4  # Parallelize across 4 cores )

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: can combine fits separate runs using combine_ensembles(). useful two common scenarios: (1) ran initial analysis later want add repetitions increase M without re-running everything, (2) split work across multiple machines cluster jobs faster computation: large-scale cluster computing many nodes (e.g., 100 jobs), can combine multiple fits using Reduce():
code: # Session 1: Run on server 1 fit1 <- ensemble_hte(   Y = "exp_yrly_end", D = "treat", X = hte_covars,   data = microcredit, prop_score = "prop_score",   M = 50, K = 3 ) saveRDS(fit1, "fit_server1.rds")  # Session 2: Run on server 2 fit2 <- ensemble_hte(   Y = "exp_yrly_end", D = "treat", X = hte_covars,   data = microcredit, prop_score = "prop_score",   M = 50, K = 3 ) saveRDS(fit2, "fit_server2.rds")  # Later: Combine results fit1 <- readRDS("fit_server1.rds") fit2 <- readRDS("fit_server2.rds") fit_combined <- combine_ensembles(fit1, fit2) print(fit_combined)  # Shows M = 100 # Example: combining fits from 100 cluster jobs # Each job saved as fit_1.rds, fit_2.rds, ..., fit_100.rds  # Load all fit files fit_files <- list.files(pattern = "fit_[0-9]+\\.rds$") fits <- lapply(fit_files, readRDS)  # Combine all fits at once fit_final <- Reduce(combine_ensembles, fits) print(fit_final)  # Shows total M across all jobs

what: Example 1: Predict ITE, Analyze Treatment Effects on Different Outcome
title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: Suppose estimate treatment effects business expenses, also want know borrowers see larger effects expenses also experience larger effects household income. answers: “individuals high predicted treatment effects business expenses also experience high treatment effects household income?” top-bottom difference significant, suggests heterogeneity detected expenses “transfers” income — individuals affected outcomes.
code: # Step 1: Fit HTE model predicting treatment effects on business expenses fit_exp <- ensemble_hte(   Y = "exp_yrly_end",   D = "treat",   X = hte_covars,   data       = microcredit,   prop_score = "prop_score",   algorithms = c("lm", "grf"),   M = 3, K = 3 )  # Step 2: Analyze GATES on the DIFFERENT outcome (household income) # Groups are formed by predicted ITE on business expenses # But treatment effects are estimated on household income gates_hhinc <- gates(fit_exp, outcome = "hhinc_yrly_end") print(gates_hhinc) #> GATES Results #> ============= #>  #> Fit type: HTE (ensemble_hte) #> Outcome analyzed: hhinc_yrly_end #>   (Groups based on: exp_yrly_end ITE) #> Number of groups: 3 #> Repetitions: 2 #>  #> Group Average Treatment Effects: #>  #>   Group    Estimate   Std.Error   t value    Pr(>|t|) #>   ---------------------------------------------------- #>       1     -331.61     2963.81     -0.11       0.911  #>       2     3805.26     2480.05      1.53       0.125  #>       3     1360.64     3244.81      0.42       0.675  #>  #> Heterogeneity Tests: #>   ---------------------------------------------------- #>           Test    Estimate   Std.Error   t value    Pr(>|t|) #>   ---------------------------------------------------- #>     Top-Bottom     1692.25     4426.34      0.38       0.702  #>        Top-All     -244.88     2533.86     -0.10       0.923  #>  #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

what: Example 2: Predict Outcome (No Treatment), Analyze Treatment Effects
title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: Sometimes want use prediction (without treatment structure) see predicts benefits treatment different outcome. microcredit setting, can predict bank profitability ask whether borrowers bank rank profitable also see larger treatment effects household income. answers: “individuals high predicted bank profits different treatment effects household income?”
code: # Step 1: Predict bank profits using ensemble_pred (no treatment structure) fit_pred <- ensemble_pred(   Y    = "bank_profits_pp",   X    = profit_covars,   data = microcredit,   train_idx  = has_loan,   algorithms = c("lm", "grf"),   M = 3, K = 3 )  # Step 2: Use gates() to analyze treatment effects on household income # Groups are formed by predicted bank profits # Treatment effects are estimated on household income gates_from_pred <- gates(   fit_pred,   outcome    = "hhinc_yrly_end",   treatment  = "treat",   prop_score = "prop_score" ) print(gates_from_pred) #> GATES Results #> ============= #>  #> Fit type: Prediction (ensemble_pred) #> Outcome analyzed: hhinc_yrly_end #>   (Groups based on: bank_profits_pp predicted Y) #> Number of groups: 3 #> Repetitions: 2 #>  #> Data usage: #>   ML trained on:         650 of 1113 obs #>   Analysis evaluated on: 1113 of 1113 obs #>   Groups (3) formed on: all observations (1113 obs) #>  #> Group Average Treatment Effects: #>  #>   Group    Estimate   Std.Error   t value    Pr(>|t|) #>   ---------------------------------------------------- #>       1     -579.78     2120.12     -0.27       0.784  #>       2      390.98     1461.33      0.27       0.789  #>       3     4456.13     4270.69      1.04       0.297  #>  #> Heterogeneity Tests: #>   ---------------------------------------------------- #>           Test    Estimate   Std.Error   t value    Pr(>|t|) #>   ---------------------------------------------------- #>     Top-Bottom     5035.91     4748.43      1.06       0.289  #>        Top-All     3034.56     2971.78      1.02       0.307  #>  #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

what: Example 3: Predict ITE, Analyze Group Averages of Another Variable
title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: can also examine average level (treatment effect) different variable across groups defined predicted treatment effects. extends CLAN analysis variable.  answers: “average bank profits among borrowers high vs. low predicted treatment effects business expenses?” useful : Characterizing beneficiaries endline outcomes: Beyond CLAN (looks baseline covariates X), can examine post-treatment outcomes like employment status, health indicators, endline measures Understanding mechanisms: predicted beneficiaries Y also higher average Z, might suggest pathway treatment effects operate
code: # Step 1: Fit HTE model on business expenses fit_exp <- ensemble_hte(   Y = "exp_yrly_end",   D = "treat",   X = hte_covars,   data       = microcredit,   prop_score = "prop_score",   algorithms = c("lm", "grf"),   M = 3, K = 3 )  # Step 2: Look at average bank profits by ITE group using gavs() # Groups are formed by predicted ITE on business expenses # We see how bank profits vary across those groups gavs_profits <- gavs(fit_exp, outcome = "bank_profits_pp", subset = has_loan) print(gavs_profits) plot(gavs_profits) #> GAVS Results (Group Averages) #> ============================= #>  #> Outcome analyzed: bank_profits_pp #> Targeted outcome: exp_yrly_end #> Number of groups: 3 #> Repetitions: 2 #>  #> Data usage: #>   ML trained on:         1113 of 1113 obs #>   Analysis evaluated on: 650 of 1113 obs #>   Groups (3) formed on: all observations (1113 obs) #>  #> Group Average Outcomes (groups by predicted ITE): #>  #>   Group    Estimate   Std.Error   t value    Pr(>|t|) #>   ---------------------------------------------------- #>       1       -1.67        1.20     -1.39       0.163  #>       2        0.66        1.05      0.63       0.528  #>       3        0.99        1.24      0.80       0.426  #>  #> Heterogeneity Tests: #>   ---------------------------------------------------- #>           Test    Estimate   Std.Error   t value    Pr(>|t|) #>   ---------------------------------------------------- #>     Top-Bottom        2.65        1.72      1.54       0.123  #>        Top-All        0.99        0.99      1.01       0.314  #>  #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE

what: Example: Predict Treated-Only Outcome, Analyze Universal Outcome
title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: microcredit experiment: Bank profits (bank_profits_pp) observed borrowers received loan took — bank earns revenue disbursed loans. Household income (hhinc_yrly_end) observed everyone (treated control). want know: “individuals high predicted bank profits different treatment effects household income?”
code: # Step 1: Train prediction model on loan recipients only (where profits observed) # But generate predicted bank profits for EVERYONE fit_profits <- ensemble_pred(   Y    = "bank_profits_pp",   X    = profit_covars,   data = microcredit,   train_idx  = has_loan,  # Only train on loan recipients   algorithms = c("lm", "grf"),   M = 3, K = 3 )  # Predictions are now available for ALL 1,113 applicants (including controls) summary(fit_profits) #> Ensemble Prediction Summary #> =========================== #>  #> Call: #> ensemble_pred(data = microcredit, Y = "bank_profits_pp", X = profit_covars,  #>     train_idx = has_loan, M = 2, K = 3, algorithms = c("lm",  #>         "grf")) #>  #> Outcome:     bank_profits_pp #> Observations: 1113 #> Training obs: 650 #> Repetitions:  2 #>  #> Prediction Accuracy (averaged across 2 repetitions): #>   R-squared:         0.21 #>   RMSE:              15.21 #>   MAE:               10.94 #>   Correlation:       0.46 #>  #> Best Linear Predictor (BLP): #>   intercept:         -0.02 (SE: 0.60, p: 0.979)  #>   slope:             0.98 (SE: 0.07, p: 0.000) *** #>   -> Intercept close to 0 and slope close to 1 indicate good calibration #>  #> Group Averages (GAVS) with 3 groups: #>   Group    Estimate   Std.Error    Pr(>|t|) #>   -------------------------------------------- #>       1       -9.83        1.27       0.000 *** #>       2        0.09        1.03       0.928  #>       3        7.32        0.96       0.000 *** #>  #>   Top - Bottom:  17.15 (SE: 1.59, p: 0.000) *** #>  #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: Identify outcome observed subset (e.g., treated ) Use ensemble_pred() train_idx train subset predict Use gates() gavs() outcome parameter analyze different variable observed everyone Optionally, use treatment parameter specify treatment variable
code: # Complete workflow: fit_subset <- ensemble_pred(   Y    = "bank_profits_pp",   X    = profit_covars,   data = microcredit,   train_idx  = has_loan,  # Train only on loan recipients   algorithms = c("lm", "grf"),   M = 3, K = 3 )  # Option A: GATES - treatment effects on income by predicted profit groups gates_results <- gates(   fit_subset,   outcome    = "hhinc_yrly_end",   treatment  = "treat",   prop_score = "prop_score" )  # Option B: GAVS - average income by predicted profit groups (no treatment structure) gavs_results <- gavs(fit_subset, outcome = "hhinc_yrly_end")

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: train_idx parameter also available ensemble_hte(), useful multi-arm trials want estimate treatment effects subset predict everyone. example, three-arm trial estimate treatment effects two arms (vs. B), predict ITEs individuals including arm C:
code: # Simulate a three-arm trial: Control (C), Treatment A, Treatment B set.seed(42) N <- 600 arm <- sample(c("C", "A", "B"), N, replace = TRUE) X1 <- rnorm(N) X2 <- rnorm(N) # Treatment A has heterogeneous effects driven by X1 Y <- 1 + 0.5 * X2 + (arm == "A") * (2 + 1.5 * X1) +      (arm == "B") * 1 + rnorm(N)  three_arm <- data.frame(   Y = Y, arm = arm, X1 = X1, X2 = X2,   D_A = as.integer(arm == "A")  # Treatment indicator: A vs C )  # Estimate HTE for A vs. C, predict for everyone (including arm B) fit_A <- ensemble_hte(   Y = "Y",   D = "D_A",   X = c("X1", "X2"),   data      = three_arm,   train_idx = three_arm$arm %in% c("A", "C"),  # Train on arms A and C only   algorithms = c("lm", "grf"),   M = 3, K = 3 )  # GATES on training observations (arms A and C) gates(fit_A)  # GATES on all observations (including arm B) gates(fit_A, subset = "all") #> GATES Results #> ============= #>  #> Fit type: HTE (ensemble_hte) #> Outcome analyzed: exp_yrly_end #> Number of groups: 3 #> Repetitions: 2 #>  #> Data usage: #>   ML trained on:         161 of 1113 obs #>   Analysis evaluated on: 161 of 1113 obs #>   Groups (3) formed on: all observations (1113 obs) code: # Step 1: Also estimate HTE for B vs. C three_arm$D_B <- as.integer(three_arm$arm == "B")  fit_B <- ensemble_hte(   Y = "Y",   D = "D_B",   X = c("X1", "X2"),   data      = three_arm,   train_idx = three_arm$arm %in% c("B", "C"),  # Train on arms B and C only   algorithms = c("lm", "grf"),   M = 3, K = 3 )  # Step 2: Use fit_A to group individuals by predicted benefit from A, # then analyze treatment effects of B (on the B-vs-C comparison sample) gates_B_by_A <- gates(   fit_A,   outcome   = "Y",   treatment = "D_B",   subset    = three_arm$arm %in% c("B", "C") ) print(gates_B_by_A)  # Step 3: Conversely, group by predicted benefit from B, # then analyze treatment effects of A gates_A_by_B <- gates(   fit_B,   outcome   = "Y",   treatment = "D_A",   subset    = three_arm$arm %in% c("A", "C") ) print(gates_A_by_B)

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: ensembleHTE package supports panel data (repeated observations per individual) individual_id argument. panel data detected, two adjustments made automatically: Cluster-aware fold splitting: K-fold cross-fitting, observations individual assigned fold. prevents data leakage across folds (training one time period individual predicting another). Cluster-robust standard errors: statistical tests (GATES, BLP, GAVS, CLAN, restricted comparisons) use clustered standard errors individual level, properly accounting within-individual correlation.

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: microcredit dataset cross-sectional, example uses simulated panel data. Suppose 200 individuals observed 4 time periods, treatment assigned individual level:
code: # Simulate panel data: 200 individuals x 4 periods set.seed(123) N <- 200; T_periods <- 4 panel_data <- data.frame(   id      = rep(1:N, each = T_periods),   period  = rep(1:T_periods, times = N),   D       = rep(rbinom(N, 1, 0.5), each = T_periods),   X1      = rnorm(N * T_periods),   X2      = rnorm(N * T_periods) ) panel_data$Y <- 2 + panel_data$D * (1 + panel_data$X1) +   0.5 * panel_data$X2 + rnorm(N * T_periods)  # Fit ensemble HTE model with panel data support fit <- ensemble_hte(   Y = "Y",   D = "D",   X = c("X1", "X2"),   data = panel_data,   individual_id = "id",  # Specify the individual identifier   algorithms = c("lm", "grf"),   M = 3, K = 3 )  # All analysis functions automatically use clustered SEs gates_results <- gates(fit, n_groups = 3) blp_results <- blp(fit) clan_results <- clan(fit)

code: # Fit ensemble prediction model with panel data fit_pred <- ensemble_pred(   Y = "Y",   X = c("X1", "X2"),   data = panel_data,   individual_id = "id",   algorithms = c("lm", "grf"),   M = 3, K = 3 )  # Analysis functions automatically use clustered SEs gavs_results <- gavs(fit_pred, n_groups = 3) blp_pred_results <- blp_pred(fit_pred)

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: individual_id provided: Fold assignment: K-fold split performed individual level. NN individuals, fold contains approximately N/KN/K individuals (time periods). ensures individual never appears training test sets within fold. Standard errors: regressions use sandwich::vcovCL() clustering individual level instead sandwich::vcovHC(). CLAN uses regression-based approach cluster-robust SEs clustering active. Cross-regression covariance calculations (used gates_restricted() gavs_restricted()) also account clustering. Important: individual_id can specified column name (quoted unquoted) vector. column need included among covariates formula.

title: Complete Guide to ensembleHTE
text: Total computation time scales roughly M × K × length(algorithms). Tips faster runs: Parallelization: Set n_cores > 1 distribute repetitions across CPU cores. provides near-linear speedup. Start small: Use M = 2, K = 3, 2 algorithms verify code works scaling . Tuning: tune = TRUE can increase per-algorithm time significantly. Use final results. Cluster computing: Use combine_ensembles() split work across machines combine later. Algorithm choice: lm (OLS) fast, less powerful. grf (regression forest) often faster xgboost (especially tuning), typically faster nnet svm.

title: Quick Guide to ensembleHTE
text: analysis functions (gates, blp, clan, gavs, comparison versions) support subset argument allows evaluate results subset observations. useful : outcome observed certain observations want focus specific subpopulation need exclude certain observations analysis Important notes: - gates() blp(), subset must include observations treatment control groups - gavs(), useful outcomes observed certain units (e.g., treatment effects outcomes observable treatment) - using subset, group_on argument controls observations define group cutoffs. default (group_on = "auto") uses population used train ML model: observations ensemble_hte fits, train_idx subset ensemble_pred fits trained subset. Set group_on = "" always use observations, group_on = "analysis" form groups within analysis subset. applies gates_restricted() gavs_restricted(). - message printed indicating many observations used subset applied
code: # Subset with logical vector (e.g., only business owners) subset_biz <- microcredit$own_anybus == 1 gavs_results <- gavs(pred_fit, n_groups = 3, subset = subset_biz)  # Subset with integer indices subset_indices <- which(microcredit$lower_window == 1) gates_results <- gates(fit, n_groups = 3, subset = subset_indices)  # Explicitly use all observations blp_results <- blp(fit, subset = "all")

title: Quick Guide to ensembleHTE
text: panel longitudinal data (multiple observations per individual), use individual_id argument. ensures proper fold splitting (observations individual stay fold) uses cluster-robust standard errors. microcredit dataset cross-sectional, example uses simulated data: See complete guide vignette full panel data example.
code: # Simulate panel data: 200 individuals x 4 periods set.seed(123) N <- 200; T_periods <- 4 panel_data <- data.frame(   id     = rep(1:N, each = T_periods),   period = rep(1:T_periods, times = N),   D      = rep(rbinom(N, 1, 0.5), each = T_periods),   X1     = rnorm(N * T_periods),   X2     = rnorm(N * T_periods) ) panel_data$Y <- 2 + panel_data$D * (1 + panel_data$X1) +   0.5 * panel_data$X2 + rnorm(N * T_periods)  # Fit with individual_id for cluster-aware cross-fitting fit <- ensemble_hte(   Y = "Y",   D = "D",   X = c("X1", "X2"),   data = panel_data,   individual_id = "id",   algorithms = c("lm", "grf"),   M = 10, K = 3 )  # All downstream analyses automatically use clustered SEs gates(fit, n_groups = 3) blp(fit) clan(fit)

title: Quick Guide to ensembleHTE
text: Computation time scales M * K * length(algorithms). guidelines: Debugging: Use M = 2, K = 3, 1-2 algorithms verify code quickly Exploration: Use M = 5-10 full algorithm set Final results: Use M >= 100 valid inference (required methodology) Parallelization: Set n_cores > 1 run repetitions parallel Tuning: tune = TRUE significantly increases time; use final results

title: Introduction to ensembleHTE: Detecting Heterogeneity and Exploring Targeting Tradeoffs
text: ensembleHTE package provides practical approach heterogeneous treatment effect (HTE) analysis prediction () uses entire sample training evaluation repeated cross-fitting, (ii) combines predictions multiple machine learning algorithms single ensemble. handles treatment-effect estimation (ensemble_hte) standard prediction tasks (ensemble_pred). suite downstream analysis functions—BLP, GATES, CLAN GAVS, originally proposed Chernozhukov et al. (2025) adapted following Fava (2025)—lets test heterogeneity, characterize group, compare targeting strategies. walkthrough uses data randomized microfinance experiment Philippines, first studied Karlan Zinman (2011), individual-liability microloans randomly offered applicants. analysis inspired Athey, Fava, Karlan, Osman Zinman (2025), studies whether targeting microloans bank profitability comes cost borrower welfare. use dataset running example illustrate package’s main features three exercises: microloans affect borrowers equally? use ensemble_hte() detect characterize heterogeneous treatment effects business expenses. tradeoff bank profits borrower welfare? use ensemble_pred() predict borrowers profitable bank, ask whether borrowers also benefit —whether lending profitable borrowers comes borrowers’ expense. lender must maintain income balance? Targeting loans based predicted profitability tends steer credit toward higher-income applicants. use restricted analysis functions ask whether requiring balance across household-income quintiles meaningfully reduces lender’s ability identify profitable borrowers. Note M: Throughout walkthrough use M = 10 repetitions speed. actual applications, strongly recommend using M >= 100 ensure stable inference. can start small M verify code, scale . computation takes long time, combine_ensembles() lets split work across sessions merge results afterward.

title: Introduction to ensembleHTE: Detecting Heterogeneity and Exploring Targeting Tradeoffs
text: ensemble_hte() trains ensemble ML algorithms detect heterogeneous treatment effects. uses repeated cross-fitting—controlled M (number repetitions) K (number folds)—produce stable predictions valid standard errors. example uses M = 10 repetitions two algorithms: OLS (lm) generalized random forests (grf). discuss preferred specification end section. print() method confirms specification: summary() gives first overview results: summary reports two key diagnostics. beta1 (average treatment effect) −2,322 pesos p-value 0.59—evidence overall effect loan offers business expenses. beta2 (heterogeneity loading) 0.71 p-value 0.02—significant evidence ML model well calibrated heterogeneity: predicted treatment effects correlated actual treatment effects. GATES top-minus-bottom gap 24,800 pesos (p = 0.03), reinforcing finding. Let’s look detail.
code: set.seed(2026)  fit_hte <- ensemble_hte(   Y = "exp_yrly_end",   D = "treat",   X = hte_covars,   data       = microcredit,   prop_score = "prop_score",   algorithms = c("lm", "grf"),   M = 10,   K = 4 ) print(fit_hte) #> Ensemble HTE Fit #> ================ #>  #> Call: #> ensemble_hte(data = microcredit, Y = "exp_yrly_end", X = hte_covars,  #>     D = "treat", prop_score = "prop_score", M = 10, K = 4, algorithms = c("lm",  #>         "grf")) #>  #> Data: #>   Observations:      1113 #>   Targeted outcome:  exp_yrly_end #>   Treatment:         treat #>   Covariates:        5 #>  #> Model specification: #>   Algorithms:        lm, grf #>   Metalearner:       R-learner #>   Task type:         regression (continuous outcome) #>   R-learner method:  grf #>  #> Split-sample parameters: #>   Repetitions (M):   10 #>   Folds (K):         4 #>   Ensemble strategy: cross-validated BLP #>   Ensemble folds:    5 #>   Covariate scaling: enabled #>   Hyperparameter tuning: disabled summary(fit_hte) #> Ensemble HTE Summary #> ==================== #>  #> Call: #> ensemble_hte(data = microcredit, Y = "exp_yrly_end", X = hte_covars,  #>     D = "treat", prop_score = "prop_score", M = 10, K = 4, algorithms = c("lm",  #>         "grf")) #>  #> Outcome:     exp_yrly_end #> Treatment:   treat #> Observations: 1113 #> Repetitions:  10 #>  #> Best Linear Predictor (BLP): #>   beta1 (ATE):       -2322.49 (SE: 4320.95, p: 0.591)  #>   beta2 (HET):       0.71 (SE: 0.31, p: 0.021) * #>   -> Significant heterogeneity detected (p < 0.05) #>  #> Group Average Treatment Effects (GATES) with 3 groups: #>   Group    Estimate   Std.Error    Pr(>|t|) #>   -------------------------------------------- #>       1   -15540.33     9489.09       0.101  #>       2     -678.63     6456.99       0.916  #>       3     9226.00     5585.71       0.099 . #>  #>   Top - Bottom:  24766.33 (SE: 11108.65, p: 0.026) * #>  #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

title: Introduction to ensembleHTE: Detecting Heterogeneity and Exploring Targeting Tradeoffs
text: Best Linear Predictor (BLP) asks two questions: average treatment effect? beta1 tests whether loan offer shifts business expenses average. ML model capture real heterogeneity? beta2 tests whether predicted treatment effects correlated actual treatment effects—significant result means people genuinely affected differently. BLP: average treatment effect heterogeneity loading. , beta1 significant (p = 0.59): detectable average effect loan offers business expenses. beta2 significant (p = 0.02): ML predictions correlated actual heterogeneity, meaning effect loan offer genuinely differs across borrowers.
code: blp_res <- blp(fit_hte) print(blp_res) #> BLP Results (Best Linear Predictor of CATE) #> ============================================ #>  #> Fit type: HTE (ensemble_hte) #> Outcome analyzed: exp_yrly_end #> Repetitions: 10 #>  #> Coefficients: #>   beta1 (ATE): Average Treatment Effect #>   beta2 (HET): Heterogeneity loading (significant = ML captures heterogeneity) #>  #>     Term    Estimate   Std.Error   t value    Pr(>|t|) #>   ---------------------------------------------------- #>    beta1    -2322.49     4320.95     -0.54       0.591  #>    beta2        0.71        0.31      2.30       0.021 * #>  #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 plot(blp_res)

title: Introduction to ensembleHTE: Detecting Heterogeneity and Exploring Targeting Tradeoffs
text: GATES (Group Average Treatment Effects) sorts individuals groups based predicted treatment effect—lowest highest—estimates average effect group. treatment truly affects people differently, group-level effects vary systematically. GATES: average treatment effect predicted-effect tercile, 95% confidence intervals. bottom tercile—third borrowers predicted lowest treatment effects—shows average treatment effect −15,540 pesos, meaning loan offer estimated reduce business expenses. middle tercile near zero (−679), top tercile shows positive effect +9,226 pesos. None individually significant 5% level, surprising given sample size. pattern strongly monotonic, top-minus-bottom difference 24,766 pesos—saying average treatment effect 24,800 pesos higher top group bottom group—statistically significant (p = 0.03). consistent BLP result: treatment genuinely affects borrowers differently.

what: Who is in each group? CLAN
title: Introduction to ensembleHTE: Detecting Heterogeneity and Exploring Targeting Tradeoffs
text: Now know treatment effects vary, natural question group. CLAN (Classification Analysis) answers computing average covariate within GATES groups testing whether averages differ top bottom groups. CLAN: covariate means GATES group (scaled comparability). Compared bottom group (whose business expenses predicted decrease ), borrowers top group: significantly higher business asset values (93,546 vs. 53,070 pesos, p < 0.01). significantly fewer years business (5.3 vs. 8.6 years, p < 0.001). significantly less likely already business (62% vs. 84%, p < 0.001). slightly lower credit scores (50.5 vs. 52.1, p < 0.001). Borrowers increase expenditures response loan offer tend asset-rich relatively new business ownership—perhaps people resources using microloan start expand business. contrast, bottom group longer-established business owners higher credit scores.

text: Let’s examine calibration diagnostic closely. blp_pred() prediction analogue blp(). regresses observed outcome ML predictions tests two things: intercept close zero? , predictions systematically biased. slope close one? , predictions well calibrated: one-unit increase predicted profit corresponds one-unit increase actual profit. intercept −0.01 (significantly different zero) slope 0.99 (highly significant close one). confirms model well calibrated.

title: Ensemble Methods for Learning Features of Heterogeneous Treatment Effects
text: ensembleHTE detects characterizes heterogeneous treatment effects (HTE) randomized experiments, supports standard prediction tasks valid downstream inference. uses repeated cross-fitting—every observation used training evaluation—combines predictions multiple ML algorithms produce stable estimates avoid multiple-testing issues arise pick single best algorithm. Two main entry points: ensemble_hte() — estimate heterogeneous treatment effects. ensemble_pred() — predict outcome (treatment structure needed). suite analysis functions—blp(), gates(), gavs(), clan(), restricted counterparts—lets test heterogeneity, characterize group, compare targeting strategies. statistical foundations, see Fava (2025).

title: Ensemble Methods for Learning Features of Heterogeneous Treatment Effects
text: can install development version ensembleHTE GitHub:
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("bfava/ensembleHTE")

title: Ensemble Methods for Learning Features of Heterogeneous Treatment Effects
code: library(ensembleHTE) data(microcredit)  # 1. Fit ensemble HTE model covariates <- c("css_creditscorefinal", "lower_window",                 "own_anybus", "max_yearsinbusiness", "css_assetvalue")  fit <- ensemble_hte(   Y = "exp_yrly_end",   D = "treat",   X = covariates,   data       = microcredit,   prop_score = "prop_score",   algorithms = c("grf", "glmnet", "xgboost"),   M = 20,   K = 4 )  # 2. View results print(fit) summary(fit)  # 3. Analyze treatment effect heterogeneity blp_results   <- blp(fit)                    # Best Linear Predictor gates_results <- gates(fit, n_groups = 3)    # Group Average Treatment Effects clan_results  <- clan(fit, n_groups = 3)     # Classification Analysis  # 4. Visualize plot(gates_results) plot(clan_results)  # --- Prediction task (no treatment structure) ---  # Predict bank profits (observed only for borrowers who took a loan) has_loan <- microcredit$treat == 1 & microcredit$loan_size > 0  fit_pred <- ensemble_pred(   Y    = "bank_profits_pp",   X    = covariates,   data = microcredit,   train_idx  = has_loan,   algorithms = c("grf", "glmnet", "xgboost"),   M = 20,   K = 4 )  summary(fit_pred) gavs_results <- gavs(fit_pred, n_groups = 3) plot(gavs_results)

title: Compute CLAN (Classification Analysis) — clan
code: # \donttest{ data(microcredit) covars <- c("age", "gender", "education", "hhinc_yrly_base",             "css_creditscorefinal") dat <- microcredit[, c("hhinc_yrly_end", "treat", covars)]  fit <- ensemble_hte(   hhinc_yrly_end ~ ., treatment = treat, data = dat,   prop_score = microcredit$prop_score,   algorithms = c("lm", "grf"), M = 3, K = 3 ) #> Warning: Some propensity scores are below 0.20 or above 0.80. This package is designed for randomized controlled trials (RCTs), where propensity scores are typically well-balanced. Extreme propensity scores may indicate an observational study or a heavily unbalanced design. Please verify your experimental design. result <- clan(fit) print(result) #> CLAN Results (Classification Analysis) #> ======================================= #>  #> Outcome: hhinc_yrly_end (treatment effects) | Groups: 3 | Reps: 3 #>  #> Group Means (by predicted ITE): #>                               Top     Bottom       Else        All #>   ---------------------------------------------------------------- #>   age                       41.26      42.43      42.46      42.06 #>                            (0.45)     (0.46)     (0.32)     (0.26) #>   gender                     0.85       0.84       0.85       0.85 #>                            (0.02)     (0.02)     (0.01)     (0.01) #>   education                  1.39       1.46       1.45       1.43 #>                            (0.03)     (0.03)     (0.02)     (0.02) #>   hhinc_yrly_base        20009.68   18166.30   17018.81   18018.45 #>                          (936.51)  (1054.69)   (656.72)   (544.67) #>   css_creditscorefinal      51.62      50.53      51.17      51.32 #>                            (0.28)     (0.28)     (0.20)     (0.16) #>  #> Differences from Top Group: #>                            Top-Bot    Top-Else     Top-All #>   -------------------------------------------------------- #>   age                     -1.17.      -1.20*      -0.80*   #>                             (0.64)      (0.55)      (0.37) #>   gender                   0.00       -0.01       -0.01    #>                             (0.03)      (0.02)      (0.02) #>   education               -0.07.      -0.06.      -0.04.   #>                             (0.04)      (0.03)      (0.02) #>   hhinc_yrly_base       1843.38     2990.87**   1991.23**  #>                          (1434.09)   (1154.43)    (769.74) #>   css_creditscorefinal     1.09**      0.45        0.30    #>                             (0.40)      (0.34)      (0.23) #>  #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 plot(result)  # }

title: Combine Multiple Ensemble Objects — combine_ensembles
text: Combines multiple ensemble fit objects (either ensemble_hte_fit ensemble_pred_fit) run data specification. allows users run multiple estimation sessions (e.g., different numbers repetitions) combine increase total number repetitions M. particularly useful running computations servers clusters, can distribute work across multiple sessions nodes. example, can run M=50 repetitions 5 different servers M=10 , save results, combine single object M=50 total repetitions. function validates objects : Class (HTE pred) Sample size (n) Outcome variable name Covariate names (must match exactly) Number folds (K) Algorithms Ensemble folds Task type Metalearner (HTE objects) Treatment variable (HTE objects)

text: ... Two ensemble fit objects type (ensemble_hte_fit ensemble_pred_fit).

title: Combine Multiple Ensemble Objects — combine_ensembles
text: combined ensemble fit object class inputs,   predictions/ITEs splits concatenated across inputs.   M value updated reflect total number repetitions.

title: Combine Multiple Ensemble Objects — combine_ensembles
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ data(microcredit) covars <- c("age", "gender", "education", "hhinc_yrly_base",             "css_creditscorefinal") dat <- microcredit[, c("hhinc_yrly_end", "treat", covars)]  # Run ensemble_hte in two separate sessions (e.g., on different servers) # Session 1: fit1 <- ensemble_hte(   hhinc_yrly_end ~ ., treatment = treat, data = dat,   prop_score = microcredit$prop_score,   algorithms = c("lm", "grf"), M = 5 ) saveRDS(fit1, "fit_session1.rds")  # Session 2: fit2 <- ensemble_hte(   hhinc_yrly_end ~ ., treatment = treat, data = dat,   prop_score = microcredit$prop_score,   algorithms = c("lm", "grf"), M = 5 ) saveRDS(fit2, "fit_session2.rds")  # Later, combine the results: fit1 <- readRDS("fit_session1.rds") fit2 <- readRDS("fit_session2.rds") fit_combined <- combine_ensembles(fit1, fit2) print(fit_combined)  # Shows M = 10  # Works the same for ensemble_pred dat_pred <- microcredit[, c("bank_profits_pp", covars)] pred1 <- ensemble_pred(   bank_profits_pp ~ ., data = dat_pred,   train_idx = microcredit$loan_size > 0 & microcredit$treat == 1, M = 3 ) pred2 <- ensemble_pred(   bank_profits_pp ~ ., data = dat_pred,   train_idx = microcredit$loan_size > 0 & microcredit$treat == 1, M = 3 ) pred_combined <- combine_ensembles(pred1, pred2) } # }

what: ensembleHTE: Ensemble Methods for Heterogeneous Treatment Effect Estimation — ensembleHTE-package
title: ensembleHTE: Ensemble Methods for Heterogeneous Treatment Effect Estimation — ensembleHTE-package
text: ensembleHTE package provides tools estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTE) general predictions using ensemble learning methods. combines multiple machine learning algorithms repeated cross-fitting improve statistical power.

title: ensembleHTE: Ensemble Methods for Heterogeneous Treatment Effect Estimation — ensembleHTE-package
text: package provides two main estimation functions: ensemble_hte: Estimates individual treatment effects (ITE)     using metalearner strategies (R-learner, T-learner, S-learner, X-learner) ensemble_pred: Estimates outcome predictions using     ensemble ML algorithms Key features: Ensemble learning combining multiple ML algorithms (grf, lm, ranger, etc.) Repeated K-fold cross-fitting (M repetitions) improved power Cross-validated Best Linear Predictor (BLP) weights ensemble combination Analysis tools: GATES, BLP, CLAN, GAVS characterizing treatment effects

title: ensembleHTE: Ensemble Methods for Heterogeneous Treatment Effect Estimation — ensembleHTE-package
text: ensemble_hte: Fit ensemble HTE model metalearners ensemble_pred: Fit ensemble prediction model gates: Group Average Treatment Effects blp: Best Linear Predictor CATE clan: Classification Analysis covariate gavs: Group Averages

title: ensembleHTE: Ensemble Methods for Heterogeneous Treatment Effect Estimation — ensembleHTE-package
text: Maintainer: Bruno Fava brunovnfava@gmail.com (ORCID)

what: Fit Ensemble Heterogeneous Treatment Effects Model — ensemble_hte
title: Fit Ensemble Heterogeneous Treatment Effects Model — ensemble_hte
text: Estimates heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) using ensemble machine learning algorithms combined multiple sample splitting. function implements estimation strategy developed Fava (2025), improves statistical power averaging predictions across M repetitions K-fold cross-fitting. default, function uses R-learner metalearner strategy generalized random forest (grf) CATE estimator. function supports two interfaces: Formula interface: Specify formula, treatment, data Matrix interface: Specify Y, X, D directly     (vectors/matrices, column names looked data)

title: Fit Ensemble Heterogeneous Treatment Effects Model — ensemble_hte
code: ensemble_hte(   formula = NULL,   treatment = NULL,   data = NULL,   Y = NULL,   X = NULL,   D = NULL,   prop_score = NULL,   M = 2,   K = 3,   algorithms = c("lm", "grf"),   metalearner = c("r", "t", "s", "x"),   r_learner = "grf",   ensemble_folds = 5,   task_type = NULL,   scale_covariates = TRUE,   tune = FALSE,   tune_params = list(time = 30, cv_folds = 3, stagnation_iters = 250,     stagnation_threshold = 0.01, measure = NULL),   learner_params = NULL,   train_idx = NULL,   ensemble_strategy = c("cv", "average"),   individual_id = NULL,   n_cores = 1 )

title: Fit Ensemble Heterogeneous Treatment Effects Model — ensemble_hte
text: formula formula specifying outcome covariates (e.g., Y ~ X1 + X2 Y ~ .). Use ~ . - Z exclude variables. Required Y, X, D provided. treatment treatment variable. Must coded 0 (control) 1 (treated). Can specified : unquoted variable name: treatment = D quoted string: treatment = "D" variable containing column name: treat_col <- "D"; treatment = treat_col Ignored using matrix interface (use D parameter instead) data data.frame data.table containing variables referenced formula, Y, X, D given column names. Y Numeric vector outcomes, string column name data. Use X D alternative formula interface. X Matrix, data.frame, character vector column names   data. character vector provided, columns extracted   data. Use Y D alternative   formula interface. Example: X = c("age", "gender", "income")   X = microcredit_covariates. D Numeric vector treatment indicators (0/1), string column name data. Must contain 0s 1s. prop_score Numeric vector propensity scores (probability treatment given covariates), single string naming column data, scalar constant. Must strictly 0 1 (exclusive). NULL (default), assumes constant propensity equal sample treatment proportion (appropriate randomized experiments). M Integer. Number sample splitting repetitions (default: 2). Higher values improve stability increase computation time. K Integer. Number cross-fitting folds within repetition (default: 3). observation appears exactly one test fold per repetition. algorithms Character vector ML algorithms include ensemble. Default c("lm", "grf"). Algorithms can come two sources: grf package: Use "grf" generalized random forest     (via grf::regression_forest grf::probability_forest) mlr3 learners: algorithm available mlr3 extensions.     Specify just algorithm name without task prefix (e.g., use "ranger"     "regr.ranger"). function automatically add appropriate     prefix based task type. Common examples include: "lm": Linear regression "ranger": Random forest "glmnet": Elastic net regularization "xgboost": Gradient boosting "nnet": Neural network "kknn": K-nearest neighbors "svm": Support vector machine see available learners, run mlr3::mlr_learners$keys().     Additional learners may require installing mlr3learners     mlr3extralearners packages. metalearner Character (single value). metalearner strategy ITE estimation. Exactly one : "r" (default): R-learner Robinson transformation "t": T-learner separate models per treatment arm "s": S-learner treatment feature "x": X-learner imputed counterfactuals one metalearner can used per call. See Metalearners section detailed descriptions. r_learner Character. metalearner = "r", specifies algorithm estimating conditional average treatment effect (CATE) final stage. Default "grf" (grf::causal_forest). Can : "grf": Uses grf::causal_forest (recommended) mlr3 learner: e.g., "ranger", "xgboost", "glmnet" need algorithms list. used metalearner = "r". ensemble_folds Integer. Number folds cross-validated ensemble weight estimation (default: 5). task_type Character. Type prediction task: "regr" continuous outcomes "classif" binary outcomes. NULL (default), automatically detected outcome. scale_covariates Logical. Whether standardize non-binary numeric covariates mean 0 standard deviation 1 ML training (default: TRUE). Binary variables (0/1) scaled. original data preserved returned object. tune Logical. Whether perform hyperparameter tuning ML algorithms (default: FALSE). TRUE, uses random search early stopping. tune_params List tuning parameters. title: Fit Ensemble Heterogeneous Treatment Effects Model — ensemble_hte
code: # \donttest{ # --- HTE estimation on the Philippine microcredit experiment --- # Outcome: household income; Treatment: microloan offer data(microcredit)  # Subset of covariates for speed (full set: object microcredit_covariates) covars <- c("age", "gender", "education", "hhinc_yrly_base",             "css_creditscorefinal") dat <- microcredit[, c("hhinc_yrly_end", "treat", covars)]  fit <- ensemble_hte(   hhinc_yrly_end ~ ., treatment = treat, data = dat,   prop_score = microcredit$prop_score,   algorithms = c("lm", "grf"), M = 3, K = 3 ) #> Warning: Some propensity scores are below 0.20 or above 0.80. This package is designed for randomized controlled trials (RCTs), where propensity scores are typically well-balanced. Extreme propensity scores may indicate an observational study or a heavily unbalanced design. Please verify your experimental design. print(fit) #> Ensemble HTE Fit #> ================ #>  #> Call: #> ensemble_hte(formula = hhinc_yrly_end ~ ., treatment = treat,  #>     data = dat, prop_score = microcredit$prop_score, M = 3, K = 3,  #>     algorithms = c("lm", "grf")) #>  #> Data: #>   Observations:      1113 #>   Targeted outcome:  hhinc_yrly_end #>   Treatment:         treat #>   Covariates:        5 #>  #> Model specification: #>   Algorithms:        lm, grf #>   Metalearner:       R-learner #>   Task type:         regression (continuous outcome) #>   R-learner method:  grf #>  #> Split-sample parameters: #>   Repetitions (M):   3 #>   Folds (K):         3 #>   Ensemble strategy: cross-validated BLP #>   Ensemble folds:    5 #>   Covariate scaling: enabled #>   Hyperparameter tuning: disabled summary(fit) #> Ensemble HTE Summary #> ==================== #>  #> Call: #> ensemble_hte(formula = hhinc_yrly_end ~ ., treatment = treat,  #>     data = dat, prop_score = microcredit$prop_score, M = 3, K = 3,  #>     algorithms = c("lm", "grf")) #>  #> Outcome:     hhinc_yrly_end #> Treatment:   treat #> Observations: 1113 #> Repetitions:  3 #>  #> Best Linear Predictor (BLP): #>   beta1 (ATE):       1664.22 (SE: 1683.04, p: 0.323)  #>   beta2 (HET):       -0.63 (SE: 0.73, p: 0.386)  #>   -> No significant heterogeneity detected (p >= 0.05) #>  #> Group Average Treatment Effects (GATES) with 3 groups: #>   Group    Estimate   Std.Error    Pr(>|t|) #>   -------------------------------------------- #>       1     2835.66     2565.05       0.269  #>       2      831.96     1963.17       0.672  #>       3     1125.47     4201.18       0.789  #>  #>   Top - Bottom:  -1710.19 (SE: 4959.58, p: 0.730)  #>  #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1  # Downstream analysis gates(fit, n_groups = 3) #> GATES Results #> ============= #>  #> Fit type: HTE (ensemble_hte) #> Outcome analyzed: hhinc_yrly_end #> Number of groups: 3 #> Repetitions: 3 #>  #> Group Average Treatment Effects: #>  #>   Group    Estimate   Std.Error   t value    Pr(>|t|) #>   ---------------------------------------------------- #>       1     2835.66     2565.05      1.11       0.269  #>       2      831.96     1963.17      0.42       0.672  #>       3     1125.47     4201.18      0.27       0.789  #>  #> Heterogeneity Tests: #>   ---------------------------------------------------- #>           Test    Estimate   Std.Error   t value    Pr(>|t|) #>   ---------------------------------------------------- #>     Top-Bottom    -1710.19     4959.58     -0.34       0.730  #>        Top-All     -474.29     3008.83     -0.16       0.875  #>  #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 blp(fit) #> BLP Results (Best Linear Predictor of CATE) #> ============================================ #>  #> Fit type: HTE (ensemble_hte) #> Outcome analyzed: hhinc_yrly_end #> Repetitions: 3 #>  #> Coefficients: #>   beta1 (ATE): Average Treatment Effect #>   beta2 (HET): Heterogeneity loading (significant = ML captures heterogeneity) #>  #>     Term    Estimate   Std.Error   t value    Pr(>|t|) #>   ---------------------------------------------------- #>    beta1     1664.22     1683.04      0.99       0.323  #>    beta2       -0.63        0.73     -0.87       0.386  #>  #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 # } if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # --- Additional interface examples ---  # Matrix interface covars <- c("age", "gender", "education", "hhinc_yrly_base",             "css_creditscorefinal") fit <- ensemble_hte(   Y = microcredit$hhinc_yrly_end,   X = microcredit[, covars],   D = microcredit$treat,   prop_score = microcredit$prop_score,   algorithms = c("lm", "ranger"), M = 5, K = 5 )  # Using all covariates from the original paper dat_full <- microcredit[, c("hhinc_yrly_end", "treat", microcredit_covariates)] fit_full <- ensemble_hte(   hhinc_yrly_end ~ ., treatment = treat, data = dat_full,   prop_score = microcredit$prop_score,   algorithms = c("lm", "grf"), M = 5, K = 5 )  # Column-name interface (equivalent, no need to subset data) fit_names <- ensemble_hte(   Y = "hhinc_yrly_end",   X = microcredit_covariates,   D = "treat",   data = microcredit,   prop_score = "prop_score",   algorithms = c("lm", "grf"), M = 5, K = 5 )  # With propensity scores and X-learner fit <- ensemble_hte(   hhinc_yrly_end ~ ., treatment = treat, data = dat,   prop_score = microcredit$prop_score,   metalearner = "x" )  # With parallel processing (4 cores) fit <- ensemble_hte(   hhinc_yrly_end ~ ., treatment = treat, data = dat,   prop_score = microcredit$prop_score,   M = 10, K = 5, n_cores = 4 )  # With algorithm-specific learner parameters (mlr3 algorithms only) fit <- ensemble_hte(   hhinc_yrly_end ~ ., treatment = treat, data = dat,   algorithms = c("ranger", "glmnet", "lm"),   learner_params = list(     ranger = list(num.trees = 1000, min.node.size = 5),     glmnet = list(alpha = 0)   # ridge regression   ) )  # Panel data: individuals observed across multiple time periods # All observations for the same individual are kept in the same fold, # and downstream analyses use cluster-robust standard errors. panel_data <- data.frame(   id = rep(1:100, each = 3),   time = rep(1:3, 100),   Y = rnorm(300),   D = rbinom(300, 1, 0.5),   X1 = rnorm(300),   X2 = rnorm(300) ) fit_panel <- ensemble_hte(   formula = Y ~ X1 + X2,   treatment = D,   data = panel_data,   individual_id = id,   algorithms = c("lm", "grf"),   M = 5, K = 3 ) gates(fit_panel, n_groups = 3) blp(fit_panel) clan(fit_panel, c("X1", "X2")) } # }

title: Fit Ensemble Prediction Model — ensemble_pred
text: Predicts outcome variable Y using ensemble machine learning algorithms combined multiple sample splitting. function implements estimation strategy developed Fava (2025), improves statistical power averaging predictions across M repetitions K-fold cross-fitting. Unlike ensemble_hte estimates heterogeneous treatment effects, function performs standard prediction Y given X without treatment variable causal structure. function supports two interfaces: Formula interface: Specify formula data Matrix interface: Specify Y X directly matrix interface, Y X can provided column name(s) instead raw data. Y single string /X character vector, corresponding columns extracted data.

code: # \donttest{ # --- Predict bank profits using the microcredit data --- # Bank profits are only observed for borrowers (loan_size > 0 & treat == 1). # Use train_idx to train on borrowers, predict for the full sample. # (Athey, Fava, Karlan, Osman & Zinman, 2025) data(microcredit)  # Subset of covariates for speed (full set: microcredit_covariates) covars <- c("age", "gender", "education", "hhinc_yrly_base",             "css_creditscorefinal")  # Include hhinc_yrly_end and treat for downstream gates() analysis dat <- microcredit[, c("bank_profits_pp", "hhinc_yrly_end", "treat",                        "prop_score", covars)] f <- as.formula(paste("bank_profits_pp ~", paste(covars, collapse = " + ")))  fit <- ensemble_pred(   f, data = dat,   train_idx = microcredit$loan_size > 0 & microcredit$treat == 1,   algorithms = c("lm", "grf"), M = 3, K = 3 ) print(fit) #> Ensemble Prediction Fit #> ======================= #>  #> Call: #> ensemble_pred(formula = f, data = dat, train_idx = microcredit$loan_size >  #>     0 & microcredit$treat == 1, M = 3, K = 3, algorithms = c("lm",  #>     "grf")) #>  #> Data: #>   Observations:      1113 #>   Training obs:      650 (subset) #>   Outcome:           bank_profits_pp #>   Covariates:        5 #>  #> Model specification: #>   Algorithms:        lm, grf #>   Task type:         regression (continuous outcome) #>  #> Split-sample parameters: #>   Repetitions (M):   3 #>   Folds (K):         3 #>   Ensemble strategy: cross-validated OLS #>   Ensemble folds:    5 #>   Covariate scaling: enabled #>   Hyperparameter tuning: disabled summary(fit) #> Ensemble Prediction Summary #> =========================== #>  #> Call: #> ensemble_pred(formula = f, data = dat, train_idx = microcredit$loan_size >  #>     0 & microcredit$treat == 1, M = 3, K = 3, algorithms = c("lm",  #>     "grf")) #>  #> Outcome:     bank_profits_pp #> Observations: 1113 #> Training obs: 650 #> Repetitions:  3 #>  #> Prediction Accuracy (averaged across 3 repetitions): #>   R-squared:         0.19 #>   RMSE:              15.39 #>   MAE:               11.08 #>   Correlation:       0.44 #>  #> Best Linear Predictor (BLP): #>   intercept:         -0.00 (SE: 0.60, p: 0.995)  #>   slope:             0.98 (SE: 0.07, p: 0.000) *** #>   -> Intercept close to 0 and slope close to 1 indicate good calibration #> Note: ML model trained on 650 of 1113 observations; GAVS evaluated on 650 observations. Use subset = "all" to use all observations. #>  #> Group Averages (GAVS) with 3 groups: #>   Group    Estimate   Std.Error    Pr(>|t|) #>   -------------------------------------------- #>       1       -9.24        1.32       0.000 *** #>       2       -0.62        0.94       0.511  #>       3        7.65        0.99       0.000 *** #>  #>   Top - Bottom:  16.90 (SE: 1.65, p: 0.000) *** #>  #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1  # Can we predict bank profits? Restricted: groups formed ranking predictions within     fold within level restrict_by     variable. difference unrestricted restricted estimates computed repetition, standard errors accounting cross-covariance two regressions (since share data). final reported estimates standard errors simple averages across \(M\) repetitions.

title: ensembleHTE 0.1.1
text: Y, X, D ensemble_hte() (Y/X ensemble_pred()) now accept column name(s) addition raw data. Pass Y single string, X character vector, D single string together data columns extracted automatically (e.g., Y = "hhinc_yrly_end", X = microcredit_covariates, D = "treat", data = microcredit). Similarly, prop_score ensemble_hte() now accepts column name string (e.g., prop_score = "prop_score"). Prettier startup message clickable links (RStudio) using cli package. ensemble_news() citation("ensembleHTE") now clickable loading message. Added cli Imports.

title: ensembleHTE 0.1.1
text: Significance stars now shown summary.ensemble_hte_fit() (BLP beta1/beta2), summary.ensemble_pred_fit() (BLP intercept/slope), restricted comparison print methods (print.gates_restricted_results(), print.gavs_restricted_results()). Signif. codes legend restricted comparison print methods updated standard R format (0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1). print.clan_results() now computes column widths dynamically data, ensuring headers, values, standard errors, separator lines always properly aligned regardless value magnitude. ITE Distribution section removed summary.ensemble_hte_fit() output. Individual ITE point estimates directly interpretable; summary now focuses BLP GATES inference. Prediction Summary section removed summary.ensemble_pred_fit() output reason. returned object longer includes prediction_summary. Internal formula now rebuilt reflect actual covariates used, improving consistency Y ~ . exclusions passed. .data.table() conversion now skipped input already data.table, avoiding unnecessary copies.

title: ensembleHTE 0.1.1
text: Fixed clan() data subsetting column names differ default internal names (Y, D, etc.), occur new column-name interface. Improved error messages Y, X, D accidentally NULL (e.g., passing non-existent column like df$wrong_name). error now identifies argument NULL suggests checking column name.

title: ensembleHTE 0.1.1
text: README substantially rewritten streamlined Quick Start using real microcredit data column-name interface, including ensemble_pred() example. New walkthrough vignette (vignettes/articles/microcredit-walkthrough.Rmd) demonstrating full analysis microcredit dataset. microcredit dataset documentation updated Karlan & Zinman (2011) citation DOI.

title: ensembleHTE 0.1.0
text: Initial CRAN release. ensemble_hte(): Ensemble heterogeneous treatment effect estimation metalearners (R-learner, T-learner, S-learner, X-learner). ensemble_pred(): Ensemble prediction model general outcome prediction. combine_ensembles(): Combine multiple ensemble fits distributed computing. gates(): Group Average Treatment Effects (GATES) estimation. blp(): Best Linear Predictor CATE. clan(): Classification Analysis covariates. blp_pred(): Best Linear Predictor prediction tasks. gavs(): Group Averages prediction tasks. gates_restricted(): Compare GATES unrestricted restricted ranking. gavs_restricted(): Compare GAVS unrestricted restricted ranking. S3 methods print, summary, plot. Comprehensive documentation vignettes. Test suite.
